often_disputed.description: Charges that are frequently disputed, commonly overbilled, or often incorrect

99285.reason: Highest-tier ER visit code — hospitals often upcode to 99285 even for moderate presentations. Requires documentation of high medical decision-making.
99285.tip: Ask for the medical documentation supporting this level. If your visit was for a non-life-threatening condition, this may have been upcoded.

G0384.reason: Highest-tier ER facility fee (Level 5). Often assigned even when care was routine.
G0384.tip: Request the criteria used to assign Level 5. Compare to your actual treatment received.

80053.reason: Comprehensive metabolic panel — often ordered for physical injuries where metabolic testing has no clinical relevance.
80053.tip: Ask your doctor why a metabolic panel was medically necessary for your specific injury. If no clear reason, dispute it.

80061.reason: Lipid panel (cholesterol test) — routinely ordered alongside metabolic panels for injury patients without cardiovascular history.
80061.tip: A lipid panel for an accident patient with no cardiac risk factors is hard to justify. This is a common add-on charge.

85025.reason: Complete blood count — reasonable for many presentations, but often one of several redundant panels ordered together.
85025.tip: If you received 3+ blood panels for a physical injury with no infection or blood disorder suspected, request an explanation for each.

96365.reason: IV infusion charges — often billed even when IV was only used for a short time or only for saline.
96365.tip: Verify the drug administered and duration. Routine saline IVs are often overbilled.

96361.reason: Additional IV infusion hours — often added without clear documentation of extended infusion.
96361.tip: Each additional hour must be documented. Request nursing notes to verify.

questionable.description: Charges that may be legitimate but warrant scrutiny given context

99284.reason: High-complexity ER visit. Could be appropriate or could be upcoded from 99283.
99284.tip: Review whether your presenting condition matched high-complexity criteria.

80048.reason: Basic metabolic panel — more justified than the comprehensive version for injury, but still worth confirming relevance.
80048.tip: Ask if this panel was clinically indicated for your specific condition.

36415.reason: Blood draw fee — sometimes separately billed on top of the lab test fees.
36415.tip: Check if this was billed in addition to lab codes. In many cases it should be bundled.

A4550.reason: Surgical trays / supply charges — vague line items that are often inflated.
A4550.tip: Request an itemized breakdown of exactly what supplies were used.

96374.reason: IV push injection — verify the drug and that it was actually administered.
96374.tip: Ask for medication administration records to confirm this service was provided.

93000.reason: ECG/EKG — routine for chest pain, but often added for unrelated complaints.
93000.tip: If you were not seen for cardiac symptoms, ask why an EKG was ordered.

normal.description: Codes that are typically appropriate for their context

unbundling_patterns.description: Billing both comprehensive AND basic metabolic panels is unbundling — 80053 already includes all tests in 80048.
unbundling_patterns.action: Dispute the 80048 charge if 80053 is also billed.

unbundling_patterns.description: Three simultaneous blood panels for a trauma/injury patient is a common overbilling pattern.
unbundling_patterns.action: Request medical necessity documentation for each panel. At minimum one should be removable.

unbundling_patterns.description: Billing both the physician E&M code at highest level AND the facility fee at highest level simultaneously.
unbundling_patterns.action: Both codes can be legitimate but warrant review — both at Level 5 is a flag for upcoding.
